text: I'd like to thank you all for coming to the session on complications. Whoops, what do I do now?
This session is chaired by Amy Neville, who is a minimally invasive surgeon from Ottawa, and myself. My name is John Hagan, and I work at Humber River Hospital in Toronto.
And during the course of surgery, we are all faced with different options, and often there's no right way to manage a complication when it occurs.
But as surgeons, we are trained to manage when things don't go totally as planned.
However, sometimes it's helpful to have input from other surgeons as to what might be some options as to how to get out of a very tight situation.
Thanks, John.
So I'll just introduce the format a little bit.
What we want is for this to be quite informal.
We basically want our panelists to imagine they're sitting in the lounge waiting for the next case or in their office and they're called by a colleague for help.
We will use some kind of multiple choice questions to help the discussion, but you're certainly free to answer as you feel fit.
We do expect some disagreement among our panelists, potentially among audience members, as these are not straightforward cases and there certainly is no unique right answer for each one.
We're hoping to show five cases, possibly four, depending on how much time and how much discuss and we generate.

And I would like to also thank our panelists for being present. So we have Dr. Michael Brunt from Washington University School of Medicine, Dr. Melissa Lipinska from University of Tennessee, and Dr. Ted Truss from Dartmouth Medical Center in New Hampshire.
Dr. Ellie may also be joining us as well.
So I'll let John lead the first case.
Okay, this is our first case, and I'll give you a bit of a case history.
You can ask some questions at the end of the case after we're finished talking about it.
And so this first case, a thirty-seven year old woman, she's got a small umbilical hernia, and she's pretty skinny, like her BMI is about twenty, and she's got a symptomatic right inguinal hernia.
So my question to the panel to start with is, is she suitable for a laparoscopic inguinal repair? So maybe Michael, did you do laparoscopic hernia repairs?
Yes, I do lap inguinals. I do a lot of open inguinals as well. I think this patient could be managed with either approach.

The advantages of an open repair in a woman, or you can do it under, or a man for that matter, you can do it under local anesthesia with sedation. You could potentially, in a woman, do a primary sutured repair as opposed to a mesh repair. Now, the problem with an open mesh repair in a woman is that there's not a big cord there to protect the nerves, so you may be a little vulnerable for lingering groin pain afterwards.
But I think a laparoscopic approach is a perfectly reasonable option. You want to be careful not to use too much mesh fixation.
Most of these are indirect, and the other point is that that sac tends to be really, really intimately associated with the round ligament, so it can be difficult to separate from it.
Melissa, do you do laparoscopic hernia repairs? I do.

I'd say that my practice is probably about eighty percent laparoscopic and I would tend to say that, you know, thinking about it from myself as a thirty-seven-year-old lady, I like the idea of a laparoscopic repair primarily because of the cosmetic component of that and the ability to fix both the inguinal and the umbilical at the same time.

But I would just argue in a woman that with an open inguinal incision, that's really not going to be a visible scar, whereas an umbilical scar will be visible. So that, in my mind, is less of an advantage in this situation.

Well, she's going to get an umbilical scar anyway if you're going to fix her umbilical hernia, but I think you need a little more information. Has she had previous surgery? If she's had a lower midline for something else, then it's not worth the struggle that you may have if you're trying to do a laparoscopic repair.

I favor a tap approach, especially in women, because I don't want to be in the peritoneal cavity where you risk a... Okay, so you're going to imagine like you're sitting in the doctor's lounge, and I'm the operating surgeon, and I'm going to call you in for some advice, okay?
So here's the video.
The surgeon makes an incision directly over the one-centimeter umbilical hernia and inserts the first port under direct vision.

The surgeon divides the median umbilical ligament and separates the peritoneum, pulling it down to access the right inguinal plane. Blood is visible in the surgical field, indicating a potential issue.
And so, what's going on there?
Michael, what do you think? It looks like a retroperitoneal hematoma. Yeah, it looks like it's a retroperitoneal, which is concerning.

I doubt it's going to be from the epigastrics, because usually you'll see the blood running down from the abdominal wall. Number three would be really, really unusual, especially with that degree of bleeding. And the herniasac plane is avascular, so it's not from that. I'd be really concerned about that.

Safety principle is I think you have to be extraordinarily careful putting in a ten millimeter trocar into the abdomen under direct insertion because although uncommon, retroperitoneal vascular injuries can be lethal and they typically are not if you use a smaller trocar.

So if you're going to put one in directly, you need to at least cut the fascia first because the distance between the aorta and iliac vessels and the posterior abdominal wall in a thin person is really not that much. So you should at least divide the fascia so you can slide it in without resistance and make sure you're lifting up. So I'd really be concerned about number one in this case.

So Melissa, what's your preferred way of getting inside the abdomen for a trocar insertion? Do you use various needles? Do you use the OptiView? Direct vision? Do you do it like this, make a cut? Put it on direct vision. And that's the safest way.
What's your preferred way of getting in? So my preferred way for an inguinal, if I'm doing it laparoscopically, is a Hassan cut down with a five millimeter trocar at the umbilicus.

The surgeon elevates the fascia using coca forceps and makes a transverse incision, ensuring the preperitoneal fat is fully exposed by lifting and creating a potential space. Different methods are used depending on the surgical technique and trocar positioning.

Ted, what's your preferred way of getting in the abdomen? I mean, you do a lot of obesity surgery as well. So how do you, do you depend on the body habitus or do you do it the same way every time? No, I think you have to tailor it based on the body habitus.

And the most terrifying patients are these skinny patients that you can, with one finger, you can push down and occlude their aorta because it's so close to the skin. The other safety issue is be very careful about midline, especially midline from umbilicus cephalad, midline access in a closed fashion. That's the dangerous scenario. If you're off the midline, then a closed technique, varus needle, insufflation, and then first trocar under optic guidance is much safer. You're much less likely to enter a major... retroperitoneal vessel with that approach.

And again, in my own practice, if I'm going to do closed initial access, it's a five and not a ten, because you can create potentially damage that is irretrievable with a ten retroperitoneal vessel injury.

Okay, so you've answered those questions there, and we're going to get on to the crux of this one here. Okay, so here we are, we're looking at, and you gotta understand, this is like a fast hernia. We didn't really understand what was going on here. The resident was with me, thought that might be liver down there. Like, you really can't assess this information, and then when you realize, you can see a little, then the anesthesiologist tells you, oh, they got a problem, the blood pressure's like, sixty, and she's given her fluids, some penispan, and the pressure's coming up, but it's really hard to kind of come up on the spot with what the heck is going on here, because it's just out of the blue.
A small puncture wound is visible on the skin of a skinny woman with an umbilical hernia. The incision appears to have caused a minor laceration of the retroperitoneum.
This is where we're going to need your help, Michael and Melissa and Ted. What are you going to do next?
What's your next step? Michael? You're going to have me go first every time here? You're the past president.

So actually, before I answer, there's a story I tell our residents. In the early days of Lab Coley, there was a surgeon doing a cut down for an open Hassan insertion put the retractors in, pulled, and was kind of pushing down, and the resident cut with a knife and cut into the iliac artery. So it can happen. And you just have to be really careful. This patient has a large retroperitoneal hematoma, is hypotensive. I think you have to open the abdomen and take care of it.
Where you get into really bad problems is with delay, and then the patient starts to kind of cycle. And so I think you have to deal with this immediately.

Melissa, anything? I think what's a little different about the institution where I am is we've got a wonderful endovascular group. And I think if this were something that could be treated with an endovascular stent and you had them available in-house right there next door, I don't think that would be a bad option.

I one hundred percent agree. If that's going to delay your treatment in any way, you go for the laparotomy and you fix it. Ted, anything to add to that? I would be uncomfortable with basically an expanding retroperitoneal hematoma to just say I'm just going to sort of watch And I think if you have a vascular service that's helpful in your hospital, that's the first step.
But I'd be inclined to favor Mike's approach and open right away. Yeah, okay.

I can tell you that this actually really was my case. And when this kind of thing's happened, the first thing you think in your mind is that I've killed her. And you're not thinking clearly. You're very likely going to make some cognitive errors in your judgment. And so no matter how much you think you've got the situation under control, you should call for help. And in fact, I did that.

I got my colleague Laz Klein to come. He was operating next door. I just comment. I think what you just said is extraordinarily important because your visceral reaction to something like this is, you know, oh, my God, and your heart rate goes up. You start to breathe a little fast. Your mind is racing and thinking about even downstream events. And I think what Dr. Hagan just said is the single most important thing. This is the time. Get somebody else in the room to help with the situation.

Yeah. And, you know, Laz came in and he said, oh, this is interesting. Like, totally different approach. It wasn't his problem. And so he said, okay, well, let's get the Buckwalter retractor. It was down in our basement somewhere. We had to dig it up, dust it off somewhere. He says, in the meantime, let me call the vascular surgeon. And we put a little pressure on it. But you look at the hematoma, it's actually quite soft. And so we're watching it to see what's happening. And we can't really kind of do nothing. So we said, well, we might as well just fix her hernia while she's here.

We're waiting to see what's happening with the hematoma and for the vascular surgeon to arrive. And it didn't really get any larger. And I'm still not sure whether we did the right thing or not. But we watched it and watched it. And nothing seemed to happen. And it was kind of the vascular surgeon said, well, maybe just keep an eye on it. And she actually did OK.

But we'll talk a little bit more about the retroperitoneal hematoma in a minute. But this goes to what Michael was saying about, like, when you have these kind of things happen, if you have the ability to kind of sit back and review, you know, is there a way to prevent this? And if you grab the umbilical hernia with forceps, make the skin incision, dissect into the abdomen, and then insert the the trocar direct into the abdomen. This is a very short video on how to do that.

The surgeon uses forceps to grasp the umbilical hernia, pulling it away from the anterior abdominal wall and retroperitoneum. A small incision is made at the umbilicus, followed by dissection under direct vision. The port is then inserted into the abdomen at the midline.

Ted? Number one, I think you got lucky with this because I'm not sure I'd be comfortable to just sort of pack it. Especially if you used a sharp trocar to get in, you don't know how far you went in, even though you had a little pinhole. Maybe with a varus needle, I'd be a little bit more relaxed about just watching it. I think You don't know what the long-term thing of this is, especially in a young woman.

If she ends up with a fistula or something later and bleeds again, I'd just be a little nervous to watch it. The other thing is I was watching your trocar insertion, and is there a blunt tip on this? I didn't see the tip. Yeah, there's no tip. There's no point on it. It's just an empty trocar.
There's no point to it. So I'm just going to talk about a similar case.

This actually happened about... just a couple, a month ago or two months ago, and I was asked to go into the gynecology room. They were doing a laparoscopic tubal ligation, and they put the varus needle into the right iliac artery, and it's one of these situations, she was obese, and she asked for the long varus needle, and so the first thing, the first thing I did when I got there was I said, let's turn on the video so we can continue. have something to talk about at Sages. And there it is there. You can see a puncture wound right there. And this is a large retroperitoneal hematoma here. And the tubal ligation was a success. I'm sure you're happy to hear about that. And so we're looking at this. And we did call the vascular surgeon in. The patient was pretty stable. And you can see the hole there. But look at it.

It's pulsating. This is a... I think this is a little different than the one I showed earlier. But it is pulsating and there is the hole there. And the vascular surgeon arrived and did a laparotomy and repaired the right iliac artery.

And so the question I have for the panel on this case if they were called in to look at it, is what do you think the outcome would have been if the artery hadn't been repaired, knowing that there was a through and through hole on it? And we'll start with Ted, because he had some concerns about the way the first case was managed.

Yeah, I think even if she stabilizes my, you know, she's either going to, develop one, or more likely if she's pretty stable in the OR and you're happy with it, then she'll end up with a pseudoaneurysm in that space. Right. Okay.

Melissa? I'm going to be the person who's super gloomy and say that my fear would be the spontaneous rupture. You know, if you're sitting there watching that thing pulsatile in the operating room, I'm not going to be comfortable with her walking out of the hospital twenty-four hours later without knowing it's definitively taken care of. Great. Michael?

I agree. I'd be very worried about number one or number two at the least. And I'll just say, I don't think we talk enough about access-related complications because unlike maybe some of the other complications we have, bleeding in the course of an operation, et cetera, they can be fatal for the patient.

And a lot of times they're in young patients. And there are Even in laparoscopic cholecystectomy, they're number two on the cause of medical legal suits and payments that are made for that operation. So I think you've got to be very aggressive and proactive about managing these. Great.

And after this case, I talked to two of our vascular surgeons, and I asked them, they said, after looking at that video, there's about a hundred percent chance there's going to be a pseudoaneurysm, um, developed with, with that kind of, uh, image. They suggested that, uh, what Melissa was recommending to go to the, um, endovascular suite would be a reasonable option. Also given that, uh, she's more, she's quite obese, her BMI was thirty-seven, you avoid a laparotomy and all the problems with that.

And I think if this happened to me again, or I was involved again, that's what I would suggest. So we have some time for some questions about this. Does anyone in the audience have a question? Hi, Ajay Upadhyay from Oakland, California.

One of the options we didn't discuss was to laparoscopically explore these hematomas and do a primary stitch on either the artery or the vein. In the minimally invasive hepatobiliary literature, we routinely suture ligate vessels all the time. And vascular surgeons are more

tuned in doing endovascular surgery, they are not into, or open surgery, they are not into minimally invasive approach. So that's another option for all these cases. Yeah, we asked our vascular surgeon if we could do that.

I was happy to help him, but he said no. Well, the problem with that is you've got to get proximal and distal control, and you really have to have high-end laparoscopic suturing skills to be able to tackle something like that, and there's You know, there's other branch vessels in that region, and as it turned out, this injury was to not only the anterior side of the artery, but the posterior side, too.

It should be very difficult to get to and suture laparoscopically, so I think that would be a challenge, and what you don't want to happen is get into the hematoma, have a lot more bleeding, and now the patient's hypotensive on the table, and you're still got to do a laparotomy to get control. So I think you have to be very, very cautious about approaching that laparoscopy. But there's certainly some areas of bleeding outside of the retroperitoneal vessels where you can do just what you suggested. But I think you have to be careful in that location.

So we'll go over here for another question. The first patient, while you're watching her for two days, did you do a CTA? No. But I think if this happened again, I would do that. That's a very good idea. We really didn't ever determine where the bleeding was from. It turns out, very likely, it wasn't a major vascular injury, because she did okay, but I'm not sure that was the best way to manage it, but that's what we did at the time.

Just a follow-up on that. I think I agree you do need the comfort level to do this. minimally invasive repair of vascular injuries. But there is one plus point, when you have a, the pneumoperitoneum does help, unlike in an open case, where blood is going all over, you can't control, you just increase the pressure to about eighteen to twenty, and you have time, put a little pressure on it, with an endo-penet, and if you have a comfort level to repair it, then certainly this could be done.

And the pneumoperitoneum does help. And without getting proximal or distal control, these can be repaired. I would disagree with you that pneumoperitoneum will stop major arterial bleeding or even reduce its significance.

Nick O'Rourke from Australia. There was a celebrated case in Germany of a ten-year-old boy who was having a laparoscopic inguinal hernia repair. and the trocar went into the iliac vessel, and the surgeon opened the patient and fixed up the anterior wall. There was a posterior injury, intimal flap, dissection, and the patient lost his leg. So I think you do need vascular input for any of these major vascular injuries, and I think it's crazy to try and tackle it on your own. Okay, thank you very much.

I think we'll get on to our next case, and Amy's gonna do it. Can we have this case two put up, please? So the second case is a little bit less dramatic, but certainly has some potential for disaster. Can we go to the beginning of that case?

As I get older, I worry when the first slide says call a urologist. I've already given you a little hint of what's about to happen. So we will start. So the scenario is this. Once again, you're hopefully enjoying your coffee in the lounge.
Your colleague is performing a sigmoid resection for a colorectal cancer, and they're basically dividing the mesentery at this point, and they call you for some help. Having technical difficulties.

The surgeon elevates the sigmoid colon and prepares to dissect the sigmoid mesentery using a ligature, employing a medial to lateral approach. The mesentery is visibly hanging down as the dissection begins.

So maybe I'll just ask our panel right now, do you typically do a lateral to medial or medial to lateral approach? And if you were in this scenario, do you think this looks like an adequate visualization of the anatomy?

Maybe Dr. Brunt, I don't know if you do a lot of colorectal surgery. Yeah, I don't do lap colons. We have a colorectal section at our institution that does all these, but I'll just address the last question. The anatomy doesn't look very clear to me at all here, so I'd be a little concerned about that aspect of this.
Who on the panel does a lot of colorectal surgery, any colorectal surgery? Okay. Okay. Just to get an idea.

I do the part that covers general surgery call, and that's about all. Yeah, exactly. All right, so I'll continue showing the video. So they're dividing the mesentery here. I do agree with Dr. Brandt that I'm not sure the anatomy is quite ready for division of the mesentery yet. But as this proceeds, you'll see where the whoops moment is about to happen.

In the lower part of the screen, a tubular structure is visible, which appears to be a ureter. The structure is not bleeding but is leaking urine, indicating an injury. Further dissection is performed around the area.
That is the ureter. So what is the next step?

Dr. Truss said he wasn't happy to see the call of the urologist option, but he may choose that. Other options are leave a drain, hope for the best, and leave after you get the colon out. Do a laparotomy to help further determine what's going on, although I think it may be obvious. Or do you repair this ureter and just get on with your resection? So maybe, Dr. Truss, you gave me some hint of what you thought you might want to do.

Well, I mean, I think you're safe to always call a urologist if you're not comfortable repairing it. I don't think you should just leave a drain and get out. I think this would probably be amenable to a stent and then repair over it. But I always feel more comfortable, especially for a medical legal standpoint as well, to have that backup for you if you can. Okay.

Dr. Brent? Yeah, no, I agree. I'd like to just make a general principle statement, and that is I think that there are certain operations that require for adjacent structures that you don't want to divide or injure a positive identification before you complete your dissection. And I think if you're doing a colectomy, that's one of those operations that you should always have a positive identification of the ureter before you go through and divide the mesentery.

Another example would be adrenalectomy, which I do a lot of. Left adrenal, there should always be a positive identification of the pancreas and not just assume that that is the adrenal gland. Because I've seen several cases in which the wrong organ was removed. So I think that's statement number one. Number two. I would definitely call a urologist. This is something that I would probably be comfortable suturing laparoscopically, but I think you're also going to want to stent up that ureter, and that may be something that you don't do.

And I think also because of the potential risk of a stricture, because there's likely some additional thermal injury to that ureter, that I would definitely want to have a urologist involved. And I think the other point that's important to make is pre-surgical placement of stents can also help here. So definitely in the difficult diverticulitis patient with a lot of chronic scarring, I get my urology colleagues to put in a stent before I start on that case.

And there are a handful of different types. Everybody's got their pros and cons of different ones. We happen to have a lighted stent, sits there and flashes at you, bright colored, very, very helpful laparoscopically where you don't have the haptic feedback of being able to palpate that. Again, hindsight is always twenty twenty, but I think if you knew you were getting into a large colorectal case or you knew you were getting into a difficult diverticular case, that's something to think about. And especially if you're there and you can't identify your anatomy, as Dr. Brunt was saying ahead of time, you can't prove that the ureter is not in your way. That's something you should do before you start dividing that mesentery.

Just echo what Mike said, and a paranoid surgeon is usually a safe surgeon. And whether you're doing a gallbladder and you want to make sure that you've got everything dissected out or whether you're doing a colon resection, you've got to look for those problem areas. Yeah, agreed.
And certainly I think we saw in that case that there was no view of the ureter as that mesentery was being divided. So we'll continue on with what was done.

The video shows a guide wire being advanced laparoscopically with assistance, followed by the placement of a stent. The colorectal surgeon performs suturing with the stent in place, completing the repair. The stent remains visible in the final repair.

OK. Did you have a comment on that, Dr. Brent? I think I would have been inclined to use a monofilament absorbable, like PDS or something like that. Fine suture, a small needle, you get it on an RB-IV needle. Makes that kind of suturing a little bit easier to do, I think. Yeah, I would agree with that as well. So a few thoughts, and some of this has been touched on by our panel already on preventing ureteric injuries and colon resection.

Certainly the danger zone has to be identified. So you have to know where to expect to find your ureter and you have to actively find it. Certainly your mesentery must be well dissected and elevated off the retroperitoneum to ensure that you have that adequate view. And a lot of that can be done bluntly. Once you start using energy devices is when you start to
certainly run into trouble if you don't have the anatomy already identified. And as we've mentioned a number of times, you do have to have that ureter in view before you start division.

The surgeon performs a division of the peritoneum and thin layers using a medial to lateral approach. The sigmoid colon is retracted upwards, revealing the gonadal vessel. Careful dissection is conducted to identify the ureter, which is visually confirmed by its vermiculation.

The video shows a green arrow indicating the ureter, while a red arrow marks the safe zone of dissection above the ureter. The ureter is slightly tented with the mesentery, highlighting a potential danger area.

We just had a few more slides in that case, if we can go back. I'll just advance through them quickly. We seem to have had some technical difficulties here, but there was one or two more slides basically just reflecting those same thoughts. So that was actually the end of the case. I think it was, as I said, relatively straightforward, certainly a lot less morbid than the previous case. Are there any questions or comments from the audience?

I just have a comment. One of the things that you noticed from that video was when they were doing the dissection, they were using, you know, energy device. And before you start kind of dividing things, exactly as Michael says, you have to find the ureter first. And you shouldn't be using energy device. You shouldn't even get it out until...

you know, until you've got it properly identified. Because you can also, I mean, you can also injure the iliac vessels, iliac vein, things like that by grabbing tissue. And it's not just the ureter that's at risk here. I think there's a question.

Sent from India. I saw the video showing a continuous suture. Is it a more continuous suture or interrupted? Because we generally do, as he says, monofilament, thin sutures, generally interrupted. But in the video, it was shown as a continuous suture. It's the way you do it, or what's your opinion on that one?

So the question was about the type of suture used? And whether it is continuous or interrupted. Continuous interrupted and monofilament versus braided. Yeah, so the urologist said Vicrol was okay to use, and it was interrupted sutures. It wasn't a full thickness ureteric injury. Thank you.

But there may be some advantage to using a monofilament. Yeah, I think as several people have reflected, the more classic teaching would be a monofilament interrupted repair. If you look at that video when he's finished, it does... You can almost see it's stricturing there a little bit when he ran that continuous suture.

But I think that's why you bring the expert in and what they're comfortable with. I probably would have done what Mike said as well, the finer suture and monofilament. Yeah, and I think it's also a nice case to show some collaboration. A urologist who wasn't necessarily comfortable with laparoscopic suturing and did guide his colorectal colleague through the repair. And I think that was a pretty good advantage to the patient.
The outcome slide was that the patient did very well postoperatively. The stent was removed four weeks later with no undue consequences. So I guess we'll move on to case three.
Okay, we'll get on to the next case. And this is the case history. The BMI is sixty. She's going to have a gastric bypass. And she went through our bariatric program in Toronto.

And she's got a problem with recurring cholecystitis and biliary colic over the past six months. And she's been in the emergency room four weeks prior to her gastric bypass because of biliary colic or cholecystitis. And she really wants to have her gallbladder removed at the time of her gastric bypass.

So my question to the panel is what advice would you give her Perhaps we'll start with Ted. In a situation like this, like your BMI is sixty, she's got a, you know, she's having recurrent attacks, a fairly colic. What's the best advice for her? So, we used to routinely take The gallbladder is out all the time for every bariatric patient, and then we stopped doing that and looked back and realized that only fourteen percent of those patients actually went on to get a cholecystectomy down the road. I really tailor how sick she feels, how sick she is. If she's had significant biliary colic, multiple trips to the emergency room, and I'm concerned that she has a bad acute cholecystitis, obviously I'm going to deal with the gallbladder alone. If it looks like she's just having some colicky episodes, I feel pretty comfortable taking the gallbladder out at the same time as her bypass. So it really depends how sick she is.

Melissa, do you have any thoughts on that? I think in a general statement, I would not hesitate to take out her gallbladder at the same time of doing her bypass. I think the distinction to me would be, can her gallbladder wait until her elective scheduled bypass? If she's been in the ER multiple times and this is becoming a more and more recurrent problem, I'm not going to do her gastric bypass at two in the morning when I'm taking out her gallbladder. in a more urgent setting.

That's gonna definitely be a separated and a delayed operation. I think sort of a secondary thing is there is a debate about concomitant procedures, right? I like the idea that if you're taking her to the operating room, you can address both of these at the same time laparoscopically and kind of give her the idea of a two for one, similar to the umbilical and inguinal hernia. I think there are certain things that when you try to put them together, you may compromise your visualization, you may compromise your setup. For example, your port placement for your laparoscopic gastric bypass is not exactly the same place that you would put your ports for a cholecystectomy.

I think people need to not be afraid to either add extra ports and put a couple of extra five millimeter trocars on there to make the view what you need, but definitely if you put them together, you can't compromise what you're doing on either independent operation. I think the latter is a really important point. And the only other point that I would make is a laparoscopic cholecystectomy in a patient with a BMI of sixty is a difficult cholecystectomy.

Period. It's going to be a difficult case. The exposure is going to be difficult. You may have to do it because the patient's having a lot of symptoms. On the other hand, if their episodes are infrequent, then you may be better off to wait until the patient's lost a fair bit of weight over the next six months and then do the gallbladder then. So I think that's the consideration you have to balance. And it really depends on the individual patient and how symptomatic they are. But this would be a difficult gallbladder. Yeah.

I think the important thing is not to do the bypass first and wait with the cholecystectomy if she's having a lot of symptoms. Because when that patient's going through that rapid weight loss period, I guarantee you she's going to be back to the hospital with more complaints. Even with using, we use ActiGol on all our patients post-operatively in that rapid weight loss period. And they still, some of them still end up with symptomatic cholecystectomy that needs surgery.

If I can add just one other point. And when they come back, they may have a stone in their bowel duct, and then you can't get to it with an ERCP. Yes, absolutely. And I guess one more comment, if you don't mind. The other thing that we do on a lot of our bariatric patients right before surgery is a liquid diet. It helps shrink the size of their liver, makes it easier.
